data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Consequences Of The Trade War For BDCs
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company (BDC) sector from both the bottom-up—highlighting individual news and events—as well as the top-down—providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the second week of April.

Market Action

BDCs were down on the week by around 3%. Month-to-date, the average BDC in our coverage is down by a double-digit level, with (GAIN) outperforming.

The volatility in the sector is the highest it has been since COVID.

The traded volume of the median stock was elevated, matching the COVID level.

The median valuation in our coverage has bounced off its intraweek low. The median discount in our coverage is at a 16% level, which is well off the historic average of a zero discount. As the chart shows, it is rare for the median discount to move wider than the current level, except for exceptional drawdowns such as the GFC and COVID.

Market Themes

There are obvious and less obvious consequences of the trade war (and subsequent market meltdown) for the BDC sector. The two obvious consequences are the likely slowdown in growth and the rise in inflation, which are now penciled in by nearly all sell-side analysts, with some calling for an outright recession. A slowdown in growth will negatively impact corporates, likely lowering their earnings, raising their leverage, and making it more difficult for them to sustain their borrowings, all else equal.

The second likely impact is that the Fed may need to cut rates to respond to this growth slowdown. There is less agreement on this than the growth slowdown, in large part because the Fed does not seem to agree. Some Fed governors appear to be calling for a pause in the series of Fed rate cuts we have seen over the last half year, while others, most notably, Chair Powell, appear to view the likely rise in prices as transitory, something that the Fed may look through and focus instead on the negatives to the labor market and growth. If the Fed does cut rates, this will be a positive for borrowers, likely leading to fewer defaults at the expense of a further drop in BDC net investment income. On balance, this would likely be a good thing for BDCs, as a modest drop in income is preferable to realized losses from defaults.

There are also two less obvious consequences of the tariff war. With credit markets nearly frozen amid sharply higher volatility, banks are having trouble finalizing loan deals in their pipelines. Banks and direct lenders, particularly those that operate in the upper middle-market space, have a somewhat overlapping borrower client base. This means that if bank deals face difficulties with institutional investors hitting pause, direct lenders can often step in quickly as there is, typically, a much smaller set of decision-makers involved in private lending.

This means that one supportive factor for BDCs from here on could be a wider pool of lending opportunities. When tariffs hit markets, banks were in the process of pitching more than $12bn of debt financings for corporate buyouts. However, the usual institutional clients were much less receptive given the sharp rise in uncertainty. Unless the banks self-fund these transactions, private credit may be the only game in town.

For example, banks are nearing a mid-Apr deadline to finance the Canadian auto parts market ABC Technologies purchase of TI Fluid Systems. The loan has not yet attracted enough demand and the sale has not launched. Chuck E. Cheese is also struggling to find interest for its $660m bond to refinance debt due next year. Brookfield delayed a $2.4bn refinancing package for a Hawaiian mall and office. This dynamic is not going to make BDCs’ year, but it does help the sector find compelling opportunities, particularly for the larger players who operate in the upper middle-market space, such as (ARCC), (BXSL), (OCSL), (OBDC) and others.

The second impact we are already seeing is that deal activity is slowing down. Specifically, IPO activity is down.

As is M&A activity.

Both Morgan Stanley and JPMorgan missed estimates on their advisory fees for M&A as well as stock listings. JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon said that large and mid-sized companies seemed to be pulling back from deals anecdotally. Uncertainty will likely remain elevated for most of Q2, which will weigh on the level of deal activity. This will keep BDC fee income subdued.

Market Commentary

Interestingly, ARCC and BXSL valuations have significantly compressed, with BXSL trading only slightly above that of ARCC. This is from a peak of nearly a 15% premium that BXSL enjoyed over ARCC. The pair is worth watching as the valuation differential tends to oscillate in the range of 0 to 15%. At this point, it can make sense to rotate from ARCC to BXSL on this basis. BXSL put up a stronger total NAV return than ARCC, so the rotation makes sense on a fundamental basis as well.

Stance and Takeaways

This week we picked up a number of BDCs, taking advantage of the previous market sell-off. Specifically, we added (MSDL), GAIN, (BCSF) and others.

The trading pattern over the last couple of decades shows that the typical sector drawdown takes the median BDC valuation down to around a 15-20% discount, while extreme periods such as that of the GFC and COVID can take the average valuation by half.

There is no way of knowing before the fact what kind of drawdown we are going to get—a typical “polite” 15-20% drop that we have seen already or an “existential” kind of 50% drop. Our view is that, unlike the GFC or the COVID period, there is no fundamental imbalance in the economy as was the case in the former, nor is there an uncontrolled external shock as was the case in the latter. This suggests that today's valuations are attractive to start adding positions, and that is precisely what we have done.

Obviously, we cannot rule out further drops, particularly as so much depends on the administration's discretionary decisions rather than impersonal macro forces. However, we maintain a decent dose of drier-powder assets, and we would put those to work in higher-yielding BDCs if the sell-off continues.

data.title: Global Ship Lease: A Contrarian Bet On The World Trade Volumes
data.description:
Introduction

Global Ship Lease (GSL) owns a fleet of 71 container ships which it leases out to third parties. The company enjoys a strong backlog and the past few years have helped the company to rapidly improve the balance sheet by reducing net debt. While I initially only looked at the preferred shares, the much improved balance sheet and robust backlog now also makes the common shares quite appealing. It will however be interesting to see the management commentary on the Q1 results and the impact of the nervosity on the charter rates going forward.

2024 was once again a very strong year for the company

Global Ship Lease’s results haven’t disappointed lately, and 2024 was once again a good year for the company thanks to its robust charter book.

In the final quarter of 2024, the company reported total revenue of just over $182M, an increase of approximately 2% compared to the Q4 2023 revenue. The operating expenses did increase, but Global Ship Lease was still able to record a higher operating income on a reported basis. That’s mainly due to the fact the Q4 2023 operating income was negatively impacted by an $18.8M impairment charge which of course is non-recurring in nature. Excluding that impairment charge, the underlying operating income would have decreased by just under 2%, predominantly on the back of higher vessel operating expenses and despite a small decrease in the G&A expenses.

The net finance expenses decreased as well, and this helped the company to report a net profit of $92.6M of which about $2.4M was used to cover the preferred dividend payments. This means the net income attributable to the common shareholders of Global Ship Lease was $90.2M, and divided over the current share count of 35.45M shares outstanding, the EPS was approximately $2.54.

At the end of 2024, Global Ship Lease had approximately $167M in cash and time deposits, and considering the company had just over $145M in short-term debt and almost $539M in long-term debt, the net debt level came in at $517M at the end of last year. This represents a decrease from the $660M in net debt as of the end of 2023.

The preferred dividends remain exceptionally well covered

Global Ship Lease currently has just one series of preferred shares outstanding, and this Series B cumulative preferred capital is trading with GSL.PR.B as its ticker symbol. There are currently 4.4 million of these preferred shares outstanding, resulting in a total of just under $110M in preferred equity on the balance sheet.

The Series B preferred shares pay a preferred dividend of 8.75% per year, payable in four equal quarterly tranches of $0.546875 per preferred share. As the preferred shares are currently trading at just over $26, the current preferred dividend yield is approximately 8.4%. Keep in mind these preferred shares have been callable since 2019, and although GSL hasn’t called them yet, there still is the possibility the company may call the preferred shares, which are currently trading at a premium to the $25 principal value.

As explained in the previous section of this article, the preferred dividends enjoy an excellent coverage ratio. On top of that, when looking at the balance sheet, the strong financial performance of Global Ship Lease in the past few years has been very useful in reducing the total amount of liabilities and increasing the total amount of equity on the balance sheet.

At the end of 2024, Global Ship Lease had $2.37B in assets, backed by $1.46B in equity. We know just under $110M of the total amount of equity is preferred equity, which means the balance sheet contains approximately $1.35B in common equity which ranks junior to the preferred shares. That’s a very robust step-up from the $1.07B in common equity at the end of 2023. That’s important as the common equity on a balance sheet acts as a first cushion to absorb losses

The main risk I see for the preferred shares is a general contraction in the average daily charter fee. As the world economy is experiencing difficulties and as the tariff-talk certainly doesn’t help, it will be interesting to see the details on lease renewals and the demand for the "smaller" vessels Global Ship Lease focuses on.

As you can see in the image above, the order book to fleet ratio in the segments Global Ship Lease is active in remains quite low at just 11.3%, while several older vessels may exit the fleet in the next few years (of course if charter rates in this segment remain high, the owners of older vessels may simply decide to keep them running).

Another element I certainly appreciate about Global Ship Lease is its transparency. The company does a good job in continuously providing an overview of the current market rates for its vessel types.

Investment thesis

Given the current relatively strong charter rates for container vessels, even the common shares of Global Ship Lease could be interesting at the current share price. The caveat right now is, of course, to see how the nervosity on the world markets impacts the shipping volumes and thus the charter rates. A substantial drop in the charter rates will predominantly have a negative impact on the common shares of the company, but the preferred stock should be fine. The company has been able to reduce its break-even rate to just over $9,200/day (including interest expenses) so Global Ship Lease definitely has some wiggle room here.

I have a small long position in Global Ship Lease’s preferred shares Series B since last week, and although I initially was reluctant to add to the position as I wasn’t quite sure how the world trade volumes would be impacted, a small stink bid at just over $25/share was hit. The situation will likely remain quite volatile in the near future, so I will have to continue to monitor the charter rates.

In the past few months, I also wrote some put options on the common shares of Global Ship Lease, but all options expired out of the money. While that allowed me to pocket the option premiums, it also means I currently still don’t have a long position in Global Ship Lease. I'm however, still sitting on a cash pile (and three companies in the ESCI portfolio are currently under an active all-cash buyout offer) and increasing my exposure to Global Ship Lease, likely through a combination of common shares and preferred shares.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Don't Let This Steep Selloff Go To Waste
data.description:
UNH: A Fall Into The Abyss?

Wow! What a massive reversal for UnitedHealth Group stock (NYSE:UNH) that’s arguably one of the biggest ones out there when it comes to healthcare stocks (XLV), and a "ranking member" within the XLV ETF as the No. 2 holding just behind weight loss drugs juggernaut Eli Lilly (LLY). Yet, the more than 20% crash in a day also underscores the substantive risks appended to UnitedHealth’s managed care business model right now, weakening the market’s perception of its execution proficiency and lowering enthusiasm of its so-called “safer” harbor amidst the current market volatility.

As a result, the goodwill won in early April as investors reacted positively to the higher Medicare Advantage payment rates has been quickly reversed as UNH headed back toward its February 2025 nadir. I believe the setback has reasonably uncovered new concerns among investors about whether there could be further risks that could encumber the company’s ability to meet its overall topline guidance (which was maintained) for the full year of 2025?

In my previous UNH article, I had presented my arguments why the market was too pessimistic about its resilience. However, the initial gains from those lows were eviscerated, as the decline this week scuppered its efforts, while lifting execution risks, as UNH penciled in a markedly lower earnings outlook.

UNH: Strong And Diversified Business Model

If you are an avid UNH investor, I don’t think there is any doubt that you might have felt frustrated, and probably even disheartened. Yet, let me also use the timely opportunity to remind you that UNH isn’t a disaggregated player within the healthcare value chain.

It has a well-diversified business model centered on its massive health insurance scale and leadership, and complemented by its Optum business (PBM, tech and analytics, and value-based care). Therefore, I believe it confers the company with an unparalleled resilience among its healthcare peers, allowing the company to boast not only sector-leading profitability, but also robust margins above its MCO industry peers.

Despite that, Wall Street has also downgraded their near-term optimism on UnitedHealth’s business. Based on the analysis of its forward adjusted net margin of 5.6% (as compared to previous years), it suggests even one-time execution mishaps in UnitedHealth’s Optum Health and higher MA utilization rates could have a marked impact on its overall profitability.

Hence, it does afford some contextual insights into why UNH suffered such a massive de-rating, as investors probably didn’t envision the poor delivery of its earnings metrics. Therefore, some investors might even be apprehensive that things could have been worse, if UNH and its health insurance peers didn’t garner the increased MA payment rates? I believe such is the reminder for investors that we must scrutinize the ongoing developments very carefully to ascertain whether management’s outlook of a comeback in the second half of 2025 through 2026 can be validated.

UnitedHealth attempted to assuage and calm the market, although investors were clearly unnerved with the execution. While UnitedHealth sought to demonstrate confidence in its outlook, the regulatory changes that could affect the healthcare value chain might have unintended consequences that could impact the recovery of UNH’s business model.

While UnitedHealth’s business is assessed to be less susceptible to President Trump’s on-again, off-again tariffs, the president’s executive order on healthcare also seeks to target the PBMs or middleman’s control over pricing and profitability, which could send further shockwaves and affect UNH’s recovery.

While management highlighted that the president isn’t just focusing on UnitedHealth (but the whole value chain), it’s also clear from his executive order that PBMs are one of his main target areas as it aims to unravel and clear the fog in pricing strategies and fees adopted by the industry. In addition, one of the main aims entailed in Trump’s EO is clearly intended to “address the influence of middlemen and promote open competition.” As a result, the White House wants to “create a fairer prescription drug market that lowers costs and ensures accountability across the health care system.”

Yet, I’m also assured that UNH has been taking constructive actions to address these challenges. Actions including the 100% commercial rebate pass through, cost-based pharmacy reimbursement, and removals of authorization for selected prescription drugs are favorable measures undertaken to raise transparency and equity with consumers. Still, I believe it’s still too early for us to assess what types of regulatory actions could be upcoming that might affect the company’s return to its long-term EPS growth guidance of between 13% and 16%.

UNH: Valuations Have Dropped Markedly

With a revision down to an EPS of between $26 and $26.5 for 2025, it marks a sharp deterioration, from the $29.5 to $30 guidance range communicated previously. As a result, it’s only rational for the market to de-rate the stock to reflect these unanticipated changes, intimating that UnitedHealth’s adjusted EPS growth could drop into the negative zone this year.

While it might set the company up for easier comps in 2026 as it laps the disappointing 2025, investors must contemplate whether the headwinds that could persist, given the current ambiguities surrounding the healthcare value chain imputed to the ongoing regulatory and political challenges.

UNH’s forward earnings multiple has clearly dropped well below its 10Y average, careening into the 15.4x level as seen in the above chart. Such lows have previously attracted dip buyers to return with conviction, only surpassed by previous lows during the COVID pandemic. In other words, could investors be eager to return and give the support that UNH needs right now to bottom out?

Buy Or Sell UNH Stock?

Note that UNH buyers did return to undergird the fall in February and formed a bullish reversal in March 2025. I assess that to be a remarkable development in UNH’s uptrend bias, advocating that investors have not given up on the stock.

In other words, UNH has adroitly navigated the market’s headwinds since then, corroborating the more defensive profile of its business model. Hence, the faux pas attributed to its weak execution and markedly downgraded guidance are likely the more significant factors that implicated its decline this week.

Yet, as long as the February lows are held decisively, I believe the sharp turnaround still represents a solid opportunity for investors to return more aggressively. Moreover, its valuation multiples have also corrected adequately, veering into levels that previously attracted investors to buy the dips.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

data.title: Spartan Delta: Heading Towards More Oil Production
data.description:
Spartan Delta (OTCPK:DALXF) is part of the spinoff process that also created Logan Energy (OTCPK:LOECF) while selling a major part of the company to Veren (VRN). To this day, Spartan Delta and Logan Energy have some overlapping management. The major idea is that both companies are developing different parts of a major Canadian deposit that was known for its natural gas production. But now there are intervals on this same acreage that can be competitively produced with production that is liquids rich. This production is more valuable than dry gas production. As a result, Spartan Delta will be growing initially through margin expansion and then likely production growth as well.

Management History

This management has built and sold companies before. In this case, the recent history has been the growth of Spartan Delta followed by a combination of spinoffs and sales followed by more growth. Still, having a management that has grown and sold companies (or spun them off) before mitigates a lot of small company and growth risk. That makes this idea a safer investment idea than is the case for many companies this size.

(Note: Spartan Delta Is A Canadian Company That Reports Using Canadian Dollars Unless Otherwise Noted.)

In some ways, the spinoff of Logan Energy was in effect an expansion of the whole operation rather than the creation of a company division. Such a strategy creates a clear market focus on the purpose of both companies. It might, in the long run create a better valuation and more profits for investors.

Last Article

The last article covered the creation of the new Spartan Delta. What has now passed is the first year of operation in the current form. As with any other new operations there are a lot of one-time costs that will make quarterly comparisons a real challenge.

Probably beginning with the current year, recurring operations will dominate the latest results. Long-term, I would expect that this company will again repeat the building cycle followed by a sale, a spinoff, or some similar strategy.

As was shown before, this company has treated investors rather well. I would expect that to continue.

Fiscal Year 2024

The progress here is the increase in oil production.

As is typical for companies formed by professionals that build and sell companies, the debt level is minimal. This contrasts with a lot of investors who believe that a lot of debt is necessary for a big return.

But many of the people that build and sell companies find superior returns through operations rather than financial leverage.

As the future unfolds, there are more intervals and acreage to develop that should result in a greater percentage of liquids production. The acreage does show significant condensate production. This is the most valuable liquid to produce in Canada because Canada imports condensate to meet its needs. The product often sells at a premium to light oil. If the company is successful at finding more condensate production in addition to increasing oil production, this venture could turn out to be extremely profitable in a very unexpected way.

The coming year would appear to focus upon the growth of light oil and condensate growth as these are the most valuable products of the production mix. Actual production growth appears to be on the slow side.

It is also possible that the company will continue the history of occasional opportunistic acquisitions. This would likely occur on an irregular basis. But it would be often enough to add several percentage points to the long-term growth rate.

Company Focus

This company is focused on the Deep Basin and the Duvernay.

Going forward, this company will concentrate on the basins shown. This keeps the message simple to Mr. Market as to what the company is about. Hopefully the analysis will simplify in the future as well.

Note that management owns a significant amount of stock. This is meant to align management with the shareholder goal of making a decent return on the money that is invested.

Summary

The creation of the "new" Spartan Delta will be the latest attempt to continue the record of rewarding investors.

This type of management is very good at reducing all kinds of risks that are typical upstream and smaller company "hazards of the business". Even though this is a smaller company, management experience is a huge advantage that should make a company like this a consideration for a wide variety (including risk-averse) of investors.

For me, this has long been a strong buy consideration because of that management experience. It is a stock that can be part of a basket of similar ideas, or it can be a single position. This depends largely upon the investor.

As shown above, returns can come "all at once" and there can be years between significant returns. This stock is for those with a long-term investment view.

As with any low-priced stock or even smaller companies, the upstream business can be quite volatile. That takes a certain kind of investor to be able to handle the situation. It can make a long-term investment experience very bumpy.

Risks

The key risk here would be a loss of key personnel. Any company that has a management with the kind of experience shown here of building and selling companies can be a huge challenge to replace if it has to be done. Relatively speaking, there are not that many of these kinds of management around. Having one in a company can be a huge advantage over a typical upstream company of this size. But if, for some reason, the key personnel services are lost, it could materially set back the future of the company. The whole situation would have to be completely re-evaluated taking into account the people replacing the personnel lost.

Any upstream company is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn of those commodity prices could materially change the future company outlook.

Spartan Delta as it is currently constituted has a lack of history. It could therefore be considered a new issue by the market with the resulting volatility of a new issue until some history of operations is established.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ares Management: Only A Buy After The Recession As Private Credit Set For Storm
data.description:
Ares Management (NYSE:ARES) has dipped 20% year-to-date, capping one of the worst starts of the year since the pandemic for the Los Angeles-based alternative investment manager. There is a lot to like about the business, with assets under management growing by 16% year-over-year to reach $484.4 billion as of the end of the company's fiscal 2024 fourth quarter. ARES is a private credit giant. The company operates Ares Capital (ARCC), the largest listed business development company (BDC) by market cap. Fee-paying assets under management (FPAUM) were up 12% year-over-year to reach $292.6 billion. Critically, it's because private credit formed a material 71.5% of FPAUM that ARES is now more than ever exposed to what could be set to be the greatest stress test of the industry since its boom in the aftermath of the 2008 Global Financial Crisis.

ARES last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $1.12 per share, a substantial 20.4% increase from its prior distribution, and annualized at $4.48 per share for a 3.17% dividend yield. The dividend has seen sustained growth since the pandemic with a 5-year compound annual growth rate of 23.52%, around 4x the financial sector median. The yield is attractive, albeit sitting below its sector median.

Is ARES now a buy against the dip of its common shares? No. The April 2, 2025, “Liberation Day” reciprocal tariffs have materially heightened the risk of the US falling into a recession while also ramping downstream inflation expectations. If both of these economic phenomena were to happen, we would see significant credit stress and a ramp in non-accruals across the industry. The impact would be softer capital raising and a weakening of institutional investor confidence that would force down the currently still lofty valuation multiple of ARES.

Stagflation Risks, Earnings, And Valuation Compression

The US is forecast to fall into a recession in 2025. While recession calls have in recent years seemed like an extension of the boy who cried wolf, the conditions for a recession are the highest it's ever been since the pandemic. The Atlanta Fed GDPNow model has the first quarter of 2025 GDP growth at negative 2.2%, a substantial deterioration from earlier in the year when growth north of 2% was expected. The second quarter of the year has seen the implementation of a base 10% tariff on all US imports after the 90-day suspension of reciprocal tariff rates that ranged from 46% on Cambodian imports to 145% on imports from China. The keyword is uncertainty, with some publicly listed companies suspending earnings guidance on the back of the fast-changing tariff regime. Trade between the US and China has effectively ground to a halt due to the ongoing trade war, as China's retaliation precluded it from the 90-day suspension.

ARES generated fiscal 2024 fourth-quarter revenue of $1.26 billion, up 19.4% over its year-ago comp, with a net investment income of $359 million. This was up from $345 million a year ago to drive adjusted EPS that added 2 cents over its year-ago comp to reach $1.23, annualized at $4.92 per share for a lofty price-to-earnings ratio of 28.8x. The risk here is valuation compression on a recession and a spike in inflation that creates the perfect headwinds for private credit. JP Morgan (JPM) has priced in a 60% certainty of the US falling into a recession this year, with the Yale Budget Lab estimating that US consumers face an average effective tariff rate of 28%, the highest level in well over a century since 1901. There could be at least a 0.6 percentage point rise in unemployment on the back of this by the end of 2025.

There is an argument that a pullback of banks in a recession could create conditions for private credit to fill in the gap to provide liquidity; this would, of course, be dependent on their existing portfolio not realizing the type of credit stress that would hamper new credit originations and institutional investor confidence in the funds managed by ARES. To what extent the non-investment-grade middle-market companies that comprise private credit funds will be impacted by the ongoing trade war is unknown. The forecasted recession overlaps with possible CPI and core PCE upward pressure to create a unique bottleneck for the Fed. It is harder to cut rates if inflation spikes beyond its target. The market currently expects 100 basis points of rate cuts to the end of 2025 as per the CME FedWatch Tool as the specter of a worst-case scenario for private credit looms. This would be rolling defaults from a sustained economic slowdown that grinds fundraising for new private credit funds to a halt and pushes public trading BDCs to trade at discounts to their net asset values. This uncertainty keeps me away from buying ARES even after its dip.

data.title: Tesla, Amazon To Report Earnings As Investors Watch Out For Consumer Confidence, Jobless Claims
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street looked to rebound Thursday after a tech-driven selloff dragged markets down Wednesday. Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s tariff warnings spooked investors, citing risks to inflation and employment. With the holiday-shortened week, investors’ focus turns to key data due next week, such as the U.S. Consumer Confidence on Tuesday, Mortgage Market Index, and New home sales on Wednesday. Meanwhile, Thursday will include economic reports such as Jobless Claims, Durable goods orders, existing home sales, and Atlanta Fed GDP. The United Kingdom will release its Core Retail Sales on Friday, while the U.S. will release Michigan Consumer sentiment, and Inflation expectations for 1-year and 5-year.

Tesla (TSLA), Verizon (VZ), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Boeing (BA), Amazon (AMZN), Intel (INTC), and AbbVie (ABBV) will report earnings next week.

_____________________________________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Monday, April 21: Home Bancorp (HBCP) SmartFinancial (SMBK), Comerica (CMA). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday, April 22: Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), GE Aerospace (GE), Verizon (NYSE:VZ) Lockheed Martin (NYSE:LMT). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday, April 23: Philip Morris (PM), IBM (IBM), AT&T (T), ServiceNow (NOW), Boeing (NYSE:BA). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Thursday, April 24: Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN), P&G (PG), Merck & Co. (MRK), Intel (NASDAQ:INTC). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Friday, April 25: AbbVie (NYSE:ABBV), Colgate-Palmolive (CL), Phillips 66 (PSX). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: Nvidia (NVDA) has a much higher level of implied volatility than normal based on options trading. Companies with an elevated level of short interest outstanding on them ahead of earnings include Lemonade (LMND), Gogo (GOGO), and CleanSpark (CLSK). The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Janover (JNVR), Nutex Health (NUTX), and Elong Power Holding (ELPW). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Destination XL (DXLG), SunCar (SDA) and Ironwood (IRWD).

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend next week include APA (APA), Fastenal (FAST), GE HealthCare (GEHC) and Kaiser Aluminum (KALU). Read through some dividend stock picks from Seeking Alpha analysts.

Investor Events: The Shanghai International Auto Show will begin on Wednesday and will feature model introductions from major auto brands, including Lexus (TM), Audi, Volkswagen (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTCPK:VWAGY), Mercedes-Benz (OTCPK:MBGYY) (OTCPK:MBGAF), NIO (NIO), BYD Company (OTCPK:BYDDY) (OTCPK:BYDDF), ZEEKR Intelligent (ZK), Li Auto (LI), and XPeng (XPEV). Meanwhile, the six-day American Association of Cancer Research annual meeting will begin on Friday. Companies participating include Amgen (AMGN), AstraZeneca (AZN), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Eli Lilly and Company (LLY), Gilead Sciences (GILD), Merck (MRK), Novartis (NVS), and Pfizer (PFE).

Tesla Earnings Preview: Tesla will issue its Q1 2025 earnings report next week. Investors are preparing for a mixed set of results, reflecting the electric vehicle maker's ongoing challenges and shifting market dynamics. Analysts expect Tesla to report a modest year-over-year revenue increase to around $21.7 billion, but earnings per share are forecast to decline to approximately $0.43, down from $0.45 a year ago. The anticipated drop in profitability is largely attributed to the sharp decline in vehicle deliveries during the quarter to 336,681 units, marking Tesla’s weakest quarterly delivery performance in over two years.

Amazon Earnings Preview: E-commerce giant Amazon will report its first quarter earnings this week, with analysts expecting the company to report earnings per share of $1.36 on a revenue of $155.1B. Seeking Alpha analyst Simple Investment Ideas recently downgraded AMZN from BUY to HOLD, saying that the rapid rise of Google’s Gemini could throw a major wrench in Amazon’s long-term plans even though it is relatively well positioned in the AI landscape. Analyst Paul Franke believes that a potential recession and trade war in 2025-2026 could disproportionately impact Amazon’s AWS cloud and online retail businesses, leading to substantial share weakness.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fifth Third Bancorp: Solid Q1 But Valuation Is Fair
data.description:
Shares of Fifth Third Bancorp (NASDAQ:FITB) have been hard hit in 2025 as fears about economic growth intensify with the stock down about 30% from its high. That has left shares essentially flat over the past year, wiping out what had been substantial gains. Solid Q1 results have not done much to help shares rebound as investors continue to worry about the darkening outlook. I last covered Fifth Third in October, rating shares a “hold” as I felt valuation was a bit elevated. In hindsight, a sell would have merited with the stock down 23%. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit FITB. I would not rush in to buy.

Net interest margin momentum continues

In the company’s first quarter, Fifth Third earned $0.73, which was $0.04 ahead of consensus as revenue rose by 2% to $2.1 billion. Noninterest expense was flat as FITB held compensation unchanged, thanks to a focus on headcount and cost controls over the past two years. FITB is also benefitting from re-pricing deposits and its fixed income portfolio. Net interest income was flat sequentially at $1.44 billion with a $21 million headwind from 2 fewer calendar days.

FITB generated a 3.03% net interest margin (NIM), which was up 6bps sequentially and 17bps from last year. This quarter’s NIM expansion was entirely driven by its usage of excess cash as it brings it balance sheet to a normalized place. My view has been that FITB is more likely to keep deposits stable this year than manage to grow them in a material way. Q1 results were consistent with this thinking.

As you can see below, average deposits declined $3 billion sequentially, though there is generally a seasonal decline in Q1, especially from municipalities after property taxes boost their Q4 cash holdings. Balances are down slightly from last year. I was encouraged to see a tick-up in the share of noninterest-bearing (NIB) balances. After a few years of headwinds, it does appear these balances have stabilized. Deposit costs fell by 26bps sequentially to 2.42%, a very strong pull-through of the Fed rate cut and a sign FITB is willing to let some deposits run off its book.

Loan growth has been excellent but faces headwinds

FITB can rationalize its deposits because its balance sheet still has plenty of liquidity. Its loan to core deposit is a modest 75%. FITB can run without issue at 80%, so it could essentially afford 5-6% loan growth before needing deposit growth. For the industry, loan growth has been difficult to generate, but here, the company’s performance was quite strong. Loans grew 2% sequentially in Q1. Loans are up nearly $6 billion over the past year, a very strong performance. Given a lower fed funds rate, loan yields fell by 12bps to 6.08%.

Now, FITB is guiding to ongoing loan growth (I will discuss guidance in more detail below), but I am somewhat cautious on this outlook. Over the past month, an incredible amount of economic uncertainty has been unleashed via tariffs. Trade policy has shifted dramatically, but it is unclear what the long-term tariff policy will be. Given all of this uncertainty, I believe many businesses will pause discretionary projects. This is likely to reduce loan demand, all else equal. Now, management did reiterate it feels confident it can generate loan growth, but I see more downside than upside risk to this target.

In terms of loan quality, FITB’s loan book is oriented more to business lending than commercial real estate (CRE). Over the past two years as CRE has faced significant structural challenges, I have viewed this positively. Insofar as tariffs impact the economy, the impact will likely be felt more by the corporate than the real estate sector (existing properties have minimal direct tariff exposure). While not a negative, I no longer view a business-lending oriented loan book as a competitive advantage.

FITB’s portfolio is 44% C&I and 15% commercial real estate. Importantly, just 3% is credit card, the consumer segment that will pose the greatest risk in a downturn. Additionally, half of its $18 billion CRE portfolio is owner-occupied—these loans are functionally a hybrid between a business and a CRE loan. Thus far, credit quality has been decent; of course, any impact from tariffs would not have been felt in Q1. Net charge-offs were stable at $136 million, though they are running slightly above the long-term average.

Credit quality has deteriorated, but reserves are sufficient for now

As you can see below, nonperforming loans (NPL) do continue to rise, a trend I would expect to continue. NPLs are now 0.79% of the portfolio, up 10bps. Now, it has already reserved for a worsening NPL environment with a 2.07% allowance to loan ratio. This leaves its allowance/NPL coverage right around 2.5x, a level I consider healthy.

That said, its allowance to loan ratio did decline by 1bp sequentially. Now, most of the tariff uncertainty was subsequent to quarter-end, and there is a tremendous amount of uncertainty as to the path of the economy. As such, I have expected most banks to take a wait-and-see approach with larger reserve revisions likely in Q2 if economic conditions deteriorate. Still, I would have expected FITB to modestly increase coverage. This decision to keep reserves a bit lighter contributed to the Q1 beat and is likely to be a transitory item with a weakening economy.

Elsewhere, adjusted noninterest income was $721 million, up less than 1% from last year. Wealth and asset management grew by 7% to $172 million. Partially offsetting this, capital markets activity was down 7% to $90 million. If the stock market remains below Q1 levels, we likely see a tick down in wealth management fees. Similarly, I expect macro uncertainty to weaken dealmaking and capital market activity. As such, fee income may decelerate from Q1 levels.

Capital is solid with manageable unrealized losses

FITB is well capitalized with a 10.45% common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio. As such, I view its 4.3% dividend yield as very secure. FITB executed on $225 million of Q1 buybacks. It plans to do $400-500 million of buybacks in H2. In aggregate, shares will have about a 7.3% capital return yield this year under this plan. Now, its regulatory capital number does overstate its economic capital position, which is true of nearly all regional banks.

FITB has a $44 billion investment portfolio of high-quality securities generally bought when rates were lower. As such, it has a $3.7 billion pre-tax unrealized loss. Including this loss in AOCI, capital would be 8.3% vs its 7.7% buffered minimum. Now, it can phase in AOCI losses over time, and as these bonds mature, the unrealized loss should decline. Given the portfolio has a 4.2 year duration, this will be a multiyear process.

As such, it is highly unlikely we will ever see regulatory capital as low as this number, but overall I view FITB as having a solid, but not extremely strong capital position. With its capital buffer, I view its dividend as secure, but if the economy materially worsens, its H2 buybacks would likely undershoot guidance in my view.

Guidance has modest downside risk

Alongside results, FITB provided updated guidance. As discussed earlier, I see some downside risk to loan growth, which would also pose ~1-2% risk to NII. I believe the assumed slowdown in noninterest income growth is prudent. FITB is also assuming charge-offs are essentially flat to Q1 levels. Major shifts in economic activity typically take 6-18 months to impact charge-offs, so even in a recession, 2026 charge-offs are more of a risk than 2025. NCOs could be a bit higher than guidance, but likely only modestly.

Valuation is fair, and there are better "buy the dip" opportunities

Back in October, I saw $3.50-$3.65 in 2025 EPS based on the pace of deposit growth. This had assumed no further degradation in credit losses. Last October, my base case had been ~2% US economic growth, but given recent developments, that is now overly optimistic in all likelihood. While Q1 was strong, we are likely to see an increase in provision in Q2 in my view. As such, I now see $3.30-$3.55 in earnings this year.

FITB is now trading at about 10x earnings. In this macro environment and with its solid balance sheet, I view this as a fair multiple. With the regional banks hammered almost uniformly from their highs, I would rather be in a name like Bank OZK (OZK) with a better capital position and lower multiple. At this multiple, there is no need to sell, but there are better upside opportunities. As such, I remain a hold on FITB.

data.title: ASML: Shares Could Get Way Cheaper
data.description:
ASML Holding N.V. (ASML) is down by over 6% since reporting Q1 2025 earnings. Even though the company guided $4.3 billion below the analyst consensus for FY 2025, margins for the full year were reaffirmed at 51% - 53%.

I believe the market's overreaction was mainly due to the uncertainty surrounding tariffs (both reciprocal and sectoral). CFO Roger Dassen said in the earnings call that they "are currently facing an elevated level of uncertainty surrounding tariffs, which may have both direct and indirect implications for our business".

Will tariffs on semiconductors affect EUV machines? Will these secotral tariffs add on top of the reciprocal tariffs revealed on April 2? Will the US and the EU reach an agreement before the deadline? Will the EU retailiate if no agreement is reached?

All these unanswered questions are putting pressure on the stock.

At the midpoint, the guided gross margin for Q2 is 250 bps below Q1's 54%. The Q2 revenue guidance also fell short of the Street's expectations by $1.35 billion.

Sales from China, tariffs, and the demand for EUV and DUV tools were some of the hot topics in the earnings call. Net system sales in China for FY 2025 have now been revised upwards to ~25% from the initial expectation of 20s. From a demand perspective, the company still sees this year and next year as growth periods, mainly due to rising AI demand in both logic and memory, which hasn't changed (according to the CFO) due to the tariff drama in the US.

Considering that the new administration in the US is about to clarify sectoral tariffs on semiconductors, I wouldn't jump to buy this stock yet. I believe the announcement will tank semiconductor stocks around the world, unless the tariff comes in below 25%.

I will be direct. I believe this tariff (on semis) will be short lived, similarly to the tariff on autos. I expect to see a global 10% tariff on all products, except those from China, which may face even higher tariff rates. This 10% cost will likely be passed on to US customers, including Intel, without much trouble.

That said, my Hold rating hasn't improved yet.

Strong Sales In China

Given my early concerns with the revenue in China, I was highly anticipating the earnings season to see any followups on this topic.

I have to say that I was pleasantly surprised by the management's outlook for China sales this year. The initial estimate was for low 20s of net system sales. This value was now increased to >25%. So far, it looks like export control restrictions in the region haven't affected DUV system (beyond the NXT:1950 models). So far, the company is still able to sell the 1950 and 1940 models in China, which are capable 28 nm production. As a side note, the revenue mix from China was similar to Q4 2024, representing 27% of system sales coming from this region. That's above the US, which came in at 16% in the past quarter, down from 28% in Q4 2024.

Despite the ongoing tariff drama and export control rules limiting sales in China, the company has still confirmed its long term revenue goal of EUR 44 billion to 60 billion by 2030, with a gross margin between 56 % - 60%. In my view, their 2030 target isn’t just part of a typical five year corporate plan. It seems tied to their expectations for strong results in both sales and margins from the NXE:5200 high NA EUV tools. More on this topic in the next sections.

The Impact Of Tairffs

Here’s how CFO Roger Dassen explained the drop in margins for Q2 2025:

The gross margin in the second half of the year is expected to be lower than the first half, primarily due to the expected margin dilutive effect of the revenue recognition of High-NA systems in the second half of the year, lower upgrade revenue, as well as any potential impact of tariffs.

As always, I tend to read these justifications backwards by order of importance.

First, let's put things into perspective. Q1 2025 gross margin came in at 54%. The guidance for Q2 is between 50% and 53%. At the midpoint, this is a decrease of 250 bps.

Now, let's try to gauge the impact that tariffs had on the company. As you may know, there is a tariff pause for the next ~80 days. Tariff pause doesn't mean no tariffs. The base rate is 10% for every country in the infamous list presented on Liberation day. As for Europe, Von der Leyen pointed to retaliatory tariffs, although after the Italian prime minister visit to the White House, it all points out that a deal between the US and EU will be reached.

Let's assume we have no deal, and both the US and EU set a 20% tariff on imports. Also, let's assume that the upcoming sectoral tariffs on semiconductors will disappear (similarly to what may happen to autos) before the 90 day pause (I have a high conviction of this scenario). What would be ASML's impact?

A quick look at the latest 20-F reveals an interesting slide where one can see ASML's supply chain distribution.

As seen above, the US accounts for 27% of all suppliers. Let's assume that half of COGS last year (cost of goods sold) accounted for materials ($14.27 billion / 2 = $7.14 billion). This leads to $1.43 billion of additional expenses due a hypothetical 20% retaliatory EU tariff.

From a sales perspective, US revenue accounted for 16% of total net sales last year.

Assuming the company is able to pass 50% of the tariff to customers, the expected impact would be $452 million. That's a third compared to the tariff impact on importing US products into the EU.

Overall, the impact of 20% bilateral tariffs between the US and the EU would be ~$2 billion, or 21% of last year's net earnings of $9.42 billion.

This is the worst case scenario, by the way.

In a realistic scenario, we’d likely see a 10 percent tariff on US imports, and I believe that cost would be fully passed on to customers. In fact, for FY 2025, the company has reiterated their margin guidance of 51% - 53%, with "the caveat of the certainties around tariffs", according to the CFO in his prepared remarks. Furthermore, in the Q&A, the CFO said:

the burden of that [the tariff on US imports] should not be with ASML, that the burden of the tariff, that the lion's share of the tariff burden should be borne by the next element in the value chain.

Seeing this guidance for the full year, I certainly belive that the company expects the scenario mentioned above: a universal 10% tariff, which will be fully passed on to US customers (Intel).

That said, the upcoming announcement of sector specific tariffs on semiconductors will likely hurt ASML's stock. This is the main reason I don’t think it’s a good time to buy.

High-NA EUV Adoption

Let's clarify the jargon first.

High-NA stands for high numerical aperture. The higher the NA, the smaller the features you can print on a silicon wafer, which is great news if you are planning build 2nm and beyond chips. The current generation of EUV tools has a NA of ~0.33, whereas the next generation (NXE:5000/NXE5200) has ~0.55.

In terms of average selling prices per machine, the company disclosed an ASP for the high NA tools of $380 million. In the latest Q&A, management mentioned that low NA EUV ASPs (NXE:3800E/3600) are currently around €227M, with a gross margin above the the corporate gross margin (54% in Q1 2025).

That said, we won't see mass production of these machines in the near term. The CEO noted three phases for the commercialization of high-NA EUV tools. The first one, is R&D. This is where the company and its customers is today, after shipping its 5th and last NXE:5000 machine in Q1. Last year, the company was able to recognize revenue for two of these machines and for 2025, the expectation is for five more. Interestingly enough, not all 5 will be for the R&D model (i.e., the NXE:5000). Some (no specific number given) will include the NXE:5200.

The next phase includes volume production testing with the NXE:5200 machine. This could last anywhere between 2-3 years before we see the final phase: high volume production.

And once this is done, typically, a customer will be convinced that the tool can do the job, the tool is mature. And then they will use it fully for any future node in high volume manufacturing. And that, I think, we have said will come mostly '27, '28.

So far, there are three customers that have adopted these tools: Samsung, Intel, and an unnamed third customer, that I suspect may be TSMC.

Even though I see positive progress, with improvements noted by both Intel and Samsung, the company is still several years away from seeing significant results from the sale of high NA EUV tools.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I see the current uncertainty around sectoral and reciprocal tariffs weighing on the stock. On the positive side, strong China sales despite export control restrictions and solid margin guidance for FY 2025 are encouraging. On the negative side, the upcoming tariff announcement on semis could trigger another selloff next week.

The company’s long term outlook is intact, but in the near term, there is too much policy risk to justify a buy rating. I reiterate my hold rating for now until the tariff situation becomes clearer.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Don't Be Bamboozled By The Bears Again
data.description:
We ended another tumultuous week with stocks meandering for most of Thursday. The one exception was UnitedHealth (UNH), which suffered a 22% decline from an earnings shortfall, stinging the Dow Jones Industrial Average for a 500-plus point loss. Otherwise, it was a day of relative calm compared to the past two weeks, which are breathing new life into a bearish narrative that was unsuccessful in 2023, as well as 2024. The bears are at it again, but I advise not being bamboozled by their rhetoric. Despite the debacle caused by the rollout of the Trump administration's trade policies, a recession and bear market are not foregone conclusions.

The correction that most of all market strategists at the beginning of this year expected, including myself, came fast and furious after the S&P 500 clinched its last all-time high on February 19 at 6,144. It was led by an overvalued technology sector with its largest and most dominant companies, otherwise known as the Magnificent 7, experiencing several quarters of decelerating earnings growth rates. The writing was on the wall. Yet at the same time, the remaining 493 names were just starting to see earnings growth increase. This occurred with the economic backdrop of a mid-cycle slowdown on tap and orchestrated by the Federal Reserve through its increase in short-term interest rates from near zero to a peak of 5.25% over the past two years.

A soft landing was and still is in sight, as both headline and core rates for the Consumer Price Index [CPI] and personal consumption expenditures [PCE] price index have 2-handles through March of this year. While Chairman Powell was admittedly late responding to the inflationary spiral that started at the beginning of 2021, his effort to lower the inflation rate from its peak in 2022 without derailing the economic expansion and driving unemployment higher has been very successful. If it were not for the lagging decline in shelter costs, due to the way it is calculated, we would already be at 2% inflation rate.

Additionally, the rate of economic growth held above trend for the past two years, expanding 2.8% in 2024, and the economy was on a strong footing to begin 2025 with low unemployment and steady real wage growth. That said, we were expecting the rate to slow this year, due largely to the lagged impact of tighter monetary policy in 2024. Still, corporate earnings growth was expected to broaden and strengthen to double-digits in 2025.

The correction that took place late February and early March was a normal and healthy one to rid the markets of the speculative excesses in AI-related technology names and other corners of the market that were clearly getting ahead of themselves, but the average stock was not excessively valued. The huge weighting that a handful of very expensive names had on market-cap weighted indexes like the S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) made the overall market look a lot more expensive than it was on average.

The initial correction morphed into a much deeper drawdown on "Liberation Day" with the announcement of the president's reciprocal tariff program, because the tariffs were clearly not reciprocal, as advertised. A 10% correction turned into a narrow miss of a bear market with an 18.9% decline from the all-time high. Despite recovering most of the losses that occurred in the three days that followed Liberation Day, the bears would have us think that this peak to trough decline validates the same bearish narrative that has been incorrectly forecasting a recession and bear market ever since the bull was born in October 2022.

Instead of claiming that the disinflationary trend would end, or that valuations were too high, or that the economy was on the cusp of a recession, the new-found pessimism is grounded in an outlook for stagflation. Granted, the rate of economic growth will slow, as it was scheduled to do before trade policy was introduced. I will also concede that tariffs are inflationary because they increase prices for producers and consumers. Lastly, if the tariffs are implemented as proposed, they will not only increase prices and slow growth but likely lead to higher unemployment. That is the definition of stagflation, but it assumes that the most recently proposed effective tariff rate is enacted. I think most definitely it will not.

The reason is that it would be political suicide for the president, and I think he now understands this. The reports on how tariffs will burden US businesses and consumers have been relentless the past two weeks. Consumer, business, and investor sentiment has plummeted. Members of Congress, who support the president's agenda, are looking for cover. Chairman Powell has indicated he has no intentions of lowering short-term rates, as the president is demanding, to offset the higher inflation and headwinds to growth that tariffs present. Even if Powell adhered to the president's demands, lower short-term rates would do little to offset these headwinds, as it is long-term rates that dictate borrowing costs for consumers. In fact, I have never seen the adverse consequences of a policy so widely advertised and broadly feared by the consensus as this one is today.

For this reason, I am extremely confident that we will see a significantly scaled down version of the reciprocal tariff program, which in some cases leads to no tariffs at all. Of course, this entire process will cause some long-term damage that is irreversible, but if I am correct in that tariffs are replaced with trade deals in the coming weeks, then this expansion can continue, we can avoid stagflation, as well as a deeper drawdown in the S&P 500 that results in a bear market.

data.title: Archer Aviation: I Remain Bullish
data.description:
In February, I covered Archer Aviation (ACHR) with a buy rating after the company raised additional funds. In my view, capital raises will recur until Archer Aviation has certified its Midnight eVTOL and has stabilized production. So, this is a name that is a speculative buy. The stock price has lost nearly 23% of its value since then compared to a 12.3% decline for the S&P 500. In this report, I will discuss the stock price decline, the 2024 cash burn, and update my price target.

Why Did Archer Aviation Stock Decline?

Archer Aviation did not completely follow the movements of the S&P 500. However, I have not observed any items specific to Archer Aviation that explain the sell-off. Instead, I believe that the market has positioned itself somewhat differently, and that doesn't benefit Archer Aviation.

Recession fears have risen and that could ultimately impact Archer Aviation. Currently, there is no indication that customers are rethinking their eVTOL plans, but it is likely that in case a recession does occur that some customers will. At the same time, there have been very few customers or potential customers that have outlined clear plans. So, the fear is that some of the potential sales in the pipeline may never materialize.

The other explanation is that due to the uncertainty, investors have grown somewhat more risk-adverse and that does not favor names such as Archer Aviation. We should not forget that Archer Aviation is developing an innovative product in a new transportation mode and for the development, certification and initial production, significant amounts of cash are required. These amounts are currently collected through capital raises. So, that is simply not the combination that investors like in an uncertain environment. The stock price decline, in my view, has not been driven by any change in the planning.

Furthermore, there could be an impact on the cost of goods due to the implementation of tariffs. So, the risk has risen, and the market has become more risk-averse. I believe that sums up why the stock price has declined.

Archer Aviation Bolsters Liquidity And Prepares For Service Entry

Archer Aviation ended the year with $841.3 million in cash and cash equivalents. The company burned $368.6 million in operating activities and had $82 million in investing cash burn bringing its free cash flow burn to roughly $450 million, which was fully covered through the issuance of $758.8 million of common stock. So, the company covered its cash burn and raised a substantial amount of cash to cover cash burns in 2025.

The capital raise in February would raise the pro forma cash position to more than $1 billion, which is extremely strong.

While the stock has fallen, there are positives for Archer Aviation:

  • Archer Aviation has commenced production of its first Midnight aircraft with plans to produce a total of 10 units this year in Covington.
  • The company is positioned well to capitalize on opportunities for eVTOLs in defense.
  • Archer Aviation has signed its first two customers for the Launch Edition program, which aims to support customers in bringing the Midnight to commercial service.
  • The company is still aiming to bring a taxi service to New York with United Airlines (UAL) this year.

So, there is a lot to be positive about. I especially like the Launch Edition signing with Ethiopian Airlines. The program is valued at $30 million and shows there are possibilities to capitalize beyond aircraft production and spare parts. Furthermore, it does show that eVTOLs can be deployed in regions with lower GDP per capita and perhaps also signals that urban air mobility can be deployed in areas where infrastructure is less advanced.

What Is Archer Aviation Stock Worth?

To calculate the stock price target for Archer Aviation, I am using a method that is based on the book value. The major moving item for the book value per share is the cash burn and the capital raises, which increase the number of shares outstanding.

To calculate the cash flows, I use a combination of the historical trends and comments from Archer Aviation, we’re not just projecting out based on current trends as shown in the trendlines. Instead, we use the dashed assumptions in the graph. For the first quarter of 2025, the company is expecting adjusted EBITDA between $95 million and $110 million.

For CapEx, the company provided the following comment during a recent earnings call:

We expect the spend on supplier non-recurring engineering costs and Archer CapEx to be down in 2025, since in 2024 we have invested heavily in this area.

This leads to a cash burn estimate of $430 million to $550 million, which is an $80 million increase at the low-end compared to my prior estimate. I am not trying to provide the rosiest picture, but implement the more demanding estimates to assess whether the stock remains attractive.

Based on these inputs, I expect that the cash position by the end of this year will be $976.3 million on the assumption that a $390 million raise from Stellantis will be finalized.

I believe that the cash per share will decline from $1.66 to $1.63, but the book value per share would remain more or less table at $1.49. The median forward P/B for industrials is 2.7x, which would bring the price target to $3.98 per share and by using the forward multiple of 5.3x for Archer Aviation, we would get to a price target of $7.95 providing 10% upside. A peer like Joby (JOBY) has a multiple of 5.1x, while Eve (EVEX) even trades at 8.7x forward P/B. So, 5.3x is high compared to other industrials, but looking at eVTOL peers it is not extremely high.

Conclusion: Archer Aviation Remains An Interesting Bet

I believe that Archer Aviation remains a fascinating stock. It is definitely not a stock that is cheap or risk-free. If you buy, you have to accept the risk that comes with it. However, while the stock price has declined, we are seeing some derisking elements as the company is getting closer to securing additional funding, and it is closer to service entry of the Midnight. That should start bringing in more and more down payments and reduce the need for capital raises.

I believe the stock is not cheap to buy now, but it might never be. So, this is a speculative buy with a $7.95 price target based on the projections made for 2025.
